text: The surgeon makes an incision on the peritoneum just above the deep ring in a case of indirect inguinal hernia.
The surgeon divides the layers of the transversalis fascia, creating the preperitoneal or extraperitoneal space.
The surgeon uses electrocautery, indicated by a green line on the right-hand side of the screen, to divide loose areolar tissue.
The surgeon maintains a precise dissection plane using a small energy source, ensuring controlled division of tissue layers.
The surgeon creates a dissection plane using sharp scissors or electrocautery, focusing on precise division rather than blunt dissection.
The surgeon creates the lateral space first, followed by the medial space, before proceeding to dissect the central zone, visually organizing the surgical field into distinct areas for dissection.

The surgical field displays an indirect hernia with the hernia sac extending into the scrotum through the deep ring. An arrow on the screen points towards the inferior epigastric vessels, located at the twelve o'clock position.
The surgeon performs medial dissection using a robotic approach, emphasizing horizontal dissection techniques rather than vertical tunneling.
The surgeon approaches the Cooper's ligament area, performing dissection to expose the pubic region from within.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection using short bursts of cautery, creating clear tissue planes with minimal blood loss.

The surgeon displays anatomical landmarks relevant to the indirect hernia repair, including the Cooper's ligament area and the inferior epigastric vessels, visible at the twelve o'clock position on the screen.
The surgeon approaches the lateral pelvic wall, where fascia and fat visibly cover the underlying vessels, preparing to address this area last in the dissection sequence.
The surgeon liberates the sac from the cord structures, carefully pushing down the vas deferens, surrounding vessels, pampiniform plexus, and gonadal vessels.
The surgeon selects an area midway between the point where the sac enters the deep ring and the opening in the peritoneum, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon begins dissecting directly onto the sac, progressively exposing and isolating it from surrounding tissues until the sac is completely visible and separated.
The surgeon identifies essential anatomical structures, focusing on the vas deferens while avoiding dissection into the scrotum, choosing to leave the distal part of the sac intact.
The surgeon divides the hernia sac, leaving the distal part intact with the patient's body, and proceeds to dissect the entire sac at the peritoneal reflection.
The surgeon approaches the peritoneal reflection, where the defect in the peritoneum is visible, marking the initiation point of the hernia.
The surgeon uses electrocautery with minimal charring visible on the tissue, applying extremely short bursts of current to achieve precise dissection.
The surgeon uses the left hand to apply traction while pointing towards the external iliac vein located medially. The fibro fatty tissue surrounding the vasculature is being dissected.
The surgeon identifies femoral nodes within the femoral canal, opting not to dissect them, while continuing with the rest of the dissection procedure.

The surgeon performs dissection above the obturator foramen, focusing on the lateral compartment. The presence of abundant adipose tissue is noted, which aids in preventing nerve injuries. No attempts are made to identify or visualize the nerves during this dissection.
The surgeon measures the distance from the deep inguinal ring to the peritoneal reflection, ensuring it is at least seven to eight centimeters to prevent the mesh from folding when the patient walks.

The surgeon makes an incision lower than the typical laparoscopic approach, utilizing the robotic platform to raise a superior flap. The dissection in the medial compartment is shown to be performed with ease.

The surgeon points out small branches to the rectus abdominis muscle. A three-dimensional large mesh by Bard BD is placed over the surgical site, with the incision visible in the middle third of the screen.

The surgeon places a mesh within the surgical field, assessing the need for further dissection. The dissection focuses on the triangle of doom, defined by the vas deferens medially, gonadal vessels laterally, and the reflected peritoneum forming the base. The iliac vessels are visible within the triangle.
The surgeon adjusts the mesh at the lateral angle, ensuring it sits beautifully within the surgical space. The mesh configuration appears to facilitate adequate scar plate formation.
The surgeon places a mesh in the groin area, ensuring it conforms to the anatomical shape with medial overlap. The mesh is evaluated for proper placement while distracting tissues are assessed.

The surgeon dissects the opposite side, revealing the transversalis fascia with its two distinct layers. Visible sutures are used for fixation and closure of the peritoneum, demonstrating the sequential approach to dissection and closure.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection to clear fibro-fatty tissue in the extraperitoneal space, creating a pouch while connecting both sides. The magnified view reveals small capillaries prominently.
The surgeon strips the transversalis fascia from the peritoneum, leaving tissue towards the groin to protect the nerves. The deep ring aspect is cleared, revealing a small developing indirect hernia.

The surgeon uses a robotic instrument to clear fat from the lateral pelvic wall, creating horizontal space for better access. The robotic approach requires careful spatial management to avoid deep tunnels, which can complicate dissection and bleeding control.
The surgeon creates horizontal zones in the surgical field, revealing the medial attachments that require further clearance before connecting the mesh to the opposite side.

The surgeon encounters fibro fatty tissue at the cupous ligament area, revealing a sliding fascia that requires removal. A small accessory obturator vessel is visible but does not hinder the dissection process.
The surgeon performs dissection around the gonadal vessels, which are clearly visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies and displays anatomical landmarks while preparing the surgical bed for mesh placement. Using articulation, a superior flap is created, mirroring the technique used on the opposite side.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to divide the fascia transversalis, creating a flap. The dissection progresses medially to connect two compartments, reinforcing the groin area.

The surgeon enters the opposite side groin through an aperture, performing dissection to connect two spaces and create a stable area with ridges for mesh placement. The dissection ensures the mesh will sit perfectly without movement, requiring a minimum extension of two to three centimeters.
The surgeon places a Bard 3D Max large soft mesh into the left groin area of a young patient with a small hernia. The mesh is inserted effortlessly into the prepared space.

The surgeon pushes the mesh into the groin area, allowing it to conform to the anatomical shape. The mesh is positioned to meet the counterpart on the opposite side, preparing for suturing. Distraction forces are applied to the peritoneum, indicating additional lateral dissection is required for optimal mesh placement. The surgeon replaces the right-sided monopolar cautery scissor with a mega suture cut instrument, enhancing the view of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a needle holder with an integrated scissor to pass a suture through Cooper's ligament, performing ergonomic adjustments for optimal suturing technique in the groin area.

The surgeon uses an instrument to pull out the extra thread, keeping the tail short for quick adjustments. The fixation is applied at the upper neurotic part of the muscle, avoiding the fleshy belly to minimize potential pain points.
The surgeon places loose sutures to secure the mesh in position, ensuring it is oriented correctly without being watertight. The mesh is fixed on the opposite side to maintain its placement.

The surgeon secures the middle end of the mesh with sutures that are not watertight, ensuring the mesh remains stable. The mesh provides lateral coverage in the groin area, with minimal medial overlap, suitable for the patient's bilateral indirect hernias.

The surgeon applies a three-point fixation technique with two sutures medially and one laterally to secure the mesh. A barbed suture from Quill is used for the closure of the peritoneum, providing a snug fit.

The surgeon repeats the suturing steps on the opposite side, employing backhand suturing techniques. The Quill suture is used, featuring a large loop at the end of the tail, facilitating easy feeding of the free end.
The surgeon reaches the middle edge of the incision using a barbed suture with memory, taking a few more bites to complete the closure of the peritoneum.
The surgeon closes the hernia sac and a small opening created during flap formation, using sutures to ensure all defects are closed. The needle is withdrawn, marking the completion of the surgery.
